text: One of Carr's most cited observations concerns the nature of memory. Human memory is not a hard drive; it is a living system that reconstructs experience each time we recall it. Psychologist Endel Tulving distinguished 'semantic memory' — factual knowledge — from 'episodic memory,' the autobiographical record of personal experience. What we remember shapes who we are. When we outsource memory to search engines, we are not simply storing facts externally; we are interrupting the biological process by which information becomes identity. Carr warns that a mind accustomed to Googling rather than remembering may retain access to data while losing the deeply consolidated understanding that only personal memory produces.
question: What distinction did psychologist Endel Tulving draw that is relevant to Carr's argument about memory and identity?

options.text: The distinction between conscious and unconscious information processing
The distinction between short-term and long-term memory storage
The distinction between semantic memory and episodic memory
The distinction between declarative and procedural knowledge
